description: Expanded AI agents and systems knowledge pack for Marion. This file covers the evolution of intelligent agents from classical rational-agent models through reinforcement learning, multi-agent systems, tool-using language-model agents, embodied agents, software agents, agent memory, planning, evaluation, orchestration, and governance. The emphasis is on architecture, capability composition, operational reliability, and safe autonomy.

architectural_patterns.name: classical_rational_agent
architectural_patterns.why_it_matters: The rational-agent framing remains the cleanest conceptual trunk for agent design and connects directly to planning, uncertainty, and action selection.

architectural_patterns.name: deliberative_planning_agent
architectural_patterns.why_it_matters: Classical planning still explains why agents need explicit action sequences, subgoals, and environment models.

architectural_patterns.name: reinforcement_learning_agent
architectural_patterns.why_it_matters: RL agents define how systems learn behavior through feedback and remain central to game-playing, control, and sequential decision-making.

architectural_patterns.name: tool_using_language_agent
architectural_patterns.why_it_matters: Modern language agents become useful when they can call tools, retrieve evidence, and interact with external systems rather than only emit text.

architectural_patterns.name: multi_agent_system
architectural_patterns.why_it_matters: Multi-agent designs matter for decomposition, distributed control, and role-based problem solving.

architectural_patterns.name: memory_reflection_agent
architectural_patterns.why_it_matters: Memory and reflection loops are what make agents feel persistent, adaptive, and capable of long-horizon execution.

foundational_papers_and_framework_refere.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation
A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models

case_study_references.title: AlphaGo
case_study_references.why_it_matters: A canonical case showing how planning, value estimation, policy learning, and search can be integrated into a high-performance decision-making agent.

case_study_references.title: Generative Agents sandbox study
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Illustrates how memory, reflection, and planning components shape believable multi-agent social behavior in simulated environments.

case_study_references.title: Voyager in Minecraft
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Demonstrates open-ended skill acquisition, curriculum generation, and reusable skill memory in an embodied setting.

case_study_references.title: AutoGen multi-agent task solving
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Useful for understanding how role-specialized agents can coordinate through structured conversation to handle complex tasks.

recommended_subtopics.why_it_matters: Persistent or semi-persistent memory is one of the major differentiators between one-shot assistants and agents capable of long-horizon execution.
Tool access is where agents become operationally useful, but also where reliability, permissions, and auditability become critical.
Complex tasks often benefit from role specialization, but coordination overhead and cascading error risk rise quickly.
Agent systems can look impressive in demos while failing badly in sustained tasks; benchmarks and operational metrics are essential.
As agents gain autonomy, the important question stops being what they can do and becomes what they are allowed to do, when, and under whose control.
Coding agents are one of the most commercially important agent forms, so they deserve explicit treatment as a distinct systems category.

notes_for_marion: Use this pack as the systems trunk that links foundations, psychology, cybersecurity, and governance into one operational picture.
Keep agent analysis architecture-first: state, memory, planning, tools, control boundaries, and evaluation.
Do not confuse fluent text generation with competent agency. Real agents require planning, environment interaction, memory discipline, and safe execution controls.
Cross-link this pack later into ethics/law for accountability and into cybersecurity for tool-use and autonomy risk.

strengths: Valid JSON and coherent top-level object structure.
Best operational pack for Marion orchestration because it includes failure modes, benchmarking, and safety controls.
issues: Manifest pack version for agentsSystems is 1.0.0 while file version is 1.1.0.
Bibliographic/source structures are present, but direct source URLs/DOIs/ISBNs are not attached in-file.
recommendedActions: Attach authoritative source URLs, DOI, ISBN, or publisher identifiers to reference entries.
Keep manifest and pack versions synchronized.
Use alias routing when layer labels differ from case-study domain labels.
Preserve bibliographic references without embedding copyrighted chapters.
